lessons.title: Design Is a Loop, Not a Straight Line
lessons.content:
Think about cooking a new recipe. You rarely nail it on the first try. You cook a version, taste it, decide it needs more salt, adjust, and cook it again. That try-taste-adjust cycle is the whole idea behind the **engineering design process**: a repeatable, step-by-step path that takes you from a problem to a working solution.

The key surprise for beginners is that this path is a **loop, not a straight line**. You do not march once from start to finish. You go around the cycle, learn something, and come back to improve. Designing a treehouse works the same way: you plan it, build a bit, notice the floor wobbles, and rethink part of the design.

The usual stages are: **understand the problem** (you already know this means pinning down what it must do, in testable terms), **imagine many possible ideas**, **choose** one to pursue, **design it in detail**, build a rough **prototype** (an early, cheap version), **test** it, **learn** from what fails, and then **iterate** (refine and repeat). "Iterate" simply means going around the loop again with what you learned.

Why does this matter? Because first attempts are *meant* to be improved. A real design loops back many times before it is good. If you expect to get everything right in one straight shot, you will be surprised, frustrated, and usually wrong. Expecting to circle back is not failure; it is how good things get made.

items.prompt: What is the engineering design process?
choices: A repeatable, step-by-step path from a problem to a working solution
A single drawing of the finished product
A rule that the first idea must always be built
A list of who is in charge of the project
items.explanation: The design process is a repeatable series of steps that carries you from a problem to a working solution. A single drawing is just one artifact, building the first idea no matter what is the opposite of good practice, and naming who is in charge is about roles, not the process itself.

items.prompt: Is the engineering design process best described as linear (a straight line) or iterative (a loop)?
choices: Linear: you finish each step once and never return to it
Iterative: you loop back to improve the design many times
Linear: it always ends at the first prototype
Neither: the order of steps does not matter at all
items.explanation: Real design is iterative: you go around the loop, learn, and come back to refine, often many times. Treating it as a straight line you finish once is the classic beginner mistake. The steps do follow a sensible order, so it is not true that order never matters.

items.prompt: Cooking a new recipe by tasting and adjusting until it is right is a good everyday picture of what?
choices: Building the final product perfectly on the very first try
Skipping testing because you already know the answer
Looping through build, test, and adjust to improve the result
Writing requirements and never changing anything afterward
items.explanation: Taste-and-adjust is exactly the build-test-improve loop at the heart of the design process. It is the opposite of getting it perfect on the first try, it clearly involves testing (tasting), and it shows things changing as you learn rather than being fixed forever.

items.prompt: In the design loop, what does it mean to 'iterate'?
choices: To delete the project and start a brand-new unrelated one
To stop all work once the first version exists
To hand the project to someone else to finish
To go around the loop again, refining the design with what you learned
items.explanation: Iterating means repeating the cycle, using what you learned to refine the design. It is not throwing everything away for an unrelated project, not stopping at the first version, and not handing the work off; it is deliberate, informed improvement.

items.prompt: You already learned the first stage of design from requirements work. What does 'understand the problem' mainly involve?
choices: Choosing the paint color before anything else
Pinning down what the thing must do, in clear and testable terms
Building a finished version as fast as possible
Guessing the price and moving on
items.explanation: Understanding the problem means nailing down what the result must do, stated clearly enough to test later, which connects directly to the requirements work you have done. Picking paint, rushing to a finished build, or guessing a price all skip the real job of defining the need.

items.prompt: Which sequence shows the usual flow of the design loop?
choices: Build first, then decide what problem you were solving
Test, ship, then think about requirements afterward
Understand, imagine ideas, choose, design, prototype, test, learn, iterate
Pick the first idea, build it fully, and never test it
items.explanation: The usual flow is understand the problem, imagine many ideas, choose one, design it, build a prototype, test, learn, and iterate. Building before knowing the problem, leaving requirements until after shipping, or committing to the first idea with no testing all break the sensible order.

items.prompt: Planning a big event by sketching a plan, trying part of it, spotting problems, and adjusting is an example of what?
choices: A one-shot straight line with no chance to adjust
An iterative loop, where you circle back to improve
Proof that planning is a waste of time
A reason to never test anything before the event
items.explanation: Trying part of a plan, finding problems, and adjusting is looping back to improve, which is iteration. It is not a one-shot line, it does not show planning is wasteful, and it actually relies on testing parts early rather than avoiding tests.

items.prompt: Why is it healthy to expect your first attempt at a design to need changes?
choices: Because changing anything later means the project has failed
Because first attempts are meant to be improved as you learn
Because the first attempt is always the best you can do
Because good designers never test their early versions
items.explanation: Good design loops back many times, so first attempts are starting points meant to be refined as you learn. Believing change equals failure, that the first try is automatically best, or that early versions should not be tested all misunderstand how real design works.

items.prompt: A beginner says, 'I will design it perfectly on paper, build it once, and be done.' What is the main flaw in this thinking?
choices: Real design loops back to improve, so expecting one perfect straight pass is unrealistic
Nothing is wrong; design really is a single straight line
The only flaw is that paper is the wrong material to use
The flaw is spending any time understanding the problem first
items.explanation: The flaw is treating design as a single straight pass when it is really an iterative loop that improves over many cycles. Design is not a one-shot line, the issue is not the paper itself, and understanding the problem first is a strength, not a flaw.

lessons.title: Imagine Many Ideas, Then Build a Rough Prototype
lessons.content:
Imagine you need a logo for a club. If you sketch one idea and stop, you will probably miss a better one. So good designers first **diverge**: they generate lots of different ideas on purpose. Only afterward do they **converge**: they compare the options and narrow down to the one to pursue.

Diverging means widening your options before choosing. Converging means narrowing to a single choice. Planning a trip works the same way: list many possible destinations first, then pick. The trap to avoid is **picking the first idea you think of**. Your first idea is rarely your best; reaching for more ideas before deciding is what uncovers the good ones. A handy reminder: *don't fall in love with your first idea.*

Once you have chosen a direction, the next key habit is **prototyping**. A **prototype** is a rough, cheap, early version built to answer a question, not to be the final product. It can be a paper sketch of an app screen, a cardboard model of a gadget, or a small test batch before you cook for a crowd. It just has to be good enough to learn something.

Why build rough early instead of perfecting on paper? Because a prototype lets you **learn fast and find problems while they are cheap to fix**. Perfecting every detail on paper before you test feels productive, but it hides flaws until later, when they are far more expensive to undo. The motto is **fail fast, fail cheap**: discover what is wrong early, on something quick and disposable, then improve.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'diverge' early in the design process?
choices: To commit to a single idea right away
To generate many different possible ideas on purpose
To stop having ideas and start building
To delete ideas until only the first one is left
items.explanation: Diverging means deliberately widening your options by generating many ideas before you choose. Committing to one idea immediately, halting ideas to start building, or trimming down to just the first idea are all forms of converging too early or skipping the idea-generation step.

items.prompt: Why is it better to diverge (make many ideas) before you converge (pick one)?
choices: Because the first idea you think of is usually the best
Because more ideas always cost more money to build
Because generating many options first helps you find better ideas you would otherwise miss
Because picking quickly is the main goal of design
items.explanation: Exploring many options before choosing surfaces strong ideas you would miss if you stopped at the first one. The first idea is rarely the best, generating ideas is cheap (you do not build them all), and the goal is a good result, not merely a fast pick.

items.prompt: Why is 'just pick the first idea you think of' a risky habit?
choices: It usually skips better ideas you would have found by exploring more
It takes far too long compared to other methods
It always produces the cheapest possible design
It is the recommended way to do professional design
items.explanation: Grabbing the first idea tends to lock you onto a weaker option and skip better ones you would find by diverging first. It is not slow (it is too fast), it does not guarantee the cheapest design, and it is the opposite of recommended practice; remember, don't fall in love with your first idea.

items.prompt: What is a 'prototype'?
choices: The final, polished product ready to sell
A written list of requirements
A meeting where the team votes on ideas
A rough, cheap, early version built to answer a question
items.explanation: A prototype is an early, rough, inexpensive version made to learn something, not the finished product. It is not the polished final item, not a requirements list, and not a meeting; its whole point is to be quick and disposable enough to test an idea.

items.prompt: Which of these is the best example of an early prototype?
choices: A fully manufactured product shipped to stores
A paper sketch of an app screen used to test the layout
A signed contract with a supplier
A finished user manual for the product
items.explanation: A paper sketch of a screen is a cheap, rough version made just to test an idea, which is exactly a prototype. A shipped product is the final result, and a contract or a finished manual are documents, not quick test versions you build to learn early.

items.prompt: Why build a rough prototype early instead of perfecting the design on paper first?
choices: Because paper designs are always wrong and useless
Because a prototype lets you find problems early, when they are cheap to fix
Because prototypes are meant to be the final product
Because testing should only happen at the very end of a project
items.explanation: A rough prototype lets you learn fast and catch problems while they are still cheap and easy to fix. Paper plans are useful but can hide flaws, a prototype is not the final product, and waiting until the very end to test is exactly what prototyping helps you avoid.

items.prompt: What does the motto 'fail fast, fail cheap' encourage you to do?
choices: Avoid ever making mistakes by planning forever
Discover problems early on something quick and disposable, then improve
Spend as much as possible perfecting the first version
Wait until the product is finished before checking if it works
items.explanation: Fail fast, fail cheap means surfacing problems early using quick, low-cost versions so mistakes are cheap to fix. Planning forever delays learning, perfecting the first version is expensive and risky, and waiting until the end to check is the costly trap this motto warns against.

items.prompt: A baker plans to cook 200 portions of a brand-new dish for an event. What is the smartest prototyping move?
choices: Cook all 200 at once and hope it tastes right
Skip tasting since the recipe is written down carefully
Make a small test batch first, taste it, and adjust before scaling up
Wait until the event to find out if guests like it
items.explanation: A small test batch is a cheap prototype: it lets the baker find and fix problems before committing to all 200 portions. Cooking everything at once, trusting the written recipe without tasting, or waiting until the event all risk a large, expensive failure that a quick test would have caught.

items.prompt: A teammate says, 'Let's not waste time on a rough mock-up; we'll perfect the whole design on paper before we build anything.' What is the best response?
choices: Agreed, perfecting everything on paper first is the safest approach
A quick prototype would actually help us find problems early, while they are cheap to fix
Agreed, prototypes are only for the final version of a product
We should just build the first idea fully and skip the mock-up
items.explanation: A rough prototype reveals problems early, when fixing them is cheap, which is exactly why it is worth a little time. Perfecting everything on paper hides flaws until they are costly, prototypes are early learning tools rather than final versions, and rushing to fully build the first idea skips the cheap learning a mock-up provides.

lessons.title: Iterate, Test Against Requirements, and the V-Model
lessons.content:
You have a loop and a prototype. This lesson is about what powers the loop: **feedback**, and how you know when you are done.

Each trip around the loop is a **build-test-learn cycle**. You build a version, test it, learn from what fails, and feed that lesson into the next build. Editing many drafts of an essay is the same: each draft is better because the last one showed you what to fix.

A crucial truth drives *when* you test: the **cost of change rises the later you catch a flaw**. A problem spotted in an early sketch is cheap to fix; the same problem found after everything is built is expensive, because more work now depends on it. This is exactly why prototyping early and looping often pays off: you catch flaws while they are still cheap.

How do you know the design is good enough? You **test it against the requirements**. Recall that a good requirement comes with an **acceptance test**, a concrete check that proves it is met. Testing against those tells you one clear thing: whether the design does what you asked, and therefore whether you are done. If a test fails, you iterate; if all pass, the design meets its requirements.

A simple picture for this is the **V-model**. You plan and design on the way *down* the left side, you build at the bottom, then you verify and test on the way *up* the right side, and **every requirement should have a matching test that checks it** (this is the **traceability** idea you met before). One more habit fits here: a **design review** is pausing to let others check your work before you commit to it, catching problems while they are still cheap.

items.prompt: What is a 'build-test-learn' cycle?
choices: Building the final product once and never testing it
A way to avoid ever changing the design
A meeting held only after the product ships
Building a version, testing it, and using what you learn to improve the next version
items.explanation: A build-test-learn cycle means you build, test, and feed the lessons into the next version, which is how iteration improves a design. Building once with no testing, avoiding change, or holding a post-ship meeting all miss the point: the cycle is about learning and refining as you go.

items.prompt: Why is fixing a flaw later in a project usually more expensive than fixing it early?
choices: Because late fixes are always free
Because more work has been built on top of the flaw, so changing it costs more
Because early fixes require buying special tools
Because the cost of fixing a flaw never changes over time
items.explanation: The later a flaw is caught, the more finished work depends on it, so undoing and reworking that costs more. Late fixes are not free, early fixes do not require special tools, and the whole point is that cost does change, rising the longer a flaw goes unnoticed.

items.prompt: A teammate claims, 'It costs the same to fix a problem now as it will after we ship.' Why is this wrong?
choices: It is correct; cost of change is flat over time
Because problems found after shipping are actually cheaper to fix
Because the cost of change rises the later a flaw is found, so fixing it after shipping costs more
Because shipping makes all problems disappear
items.explanation: The cost of change climbs over time, so a flaw fixed after shipping is more expensive than the same flaw caught early. Cost is not flat, late problems are not cheaper, and shipping does not erase problems; this is precisely why early testing and prototyping save money.

items.prompt: What does testing your design against its requirements tell you?
choices: Which team member worked the hardest
Whether the design does what you asked, and therefore whether you are done
How much the project will sell for
Only what color the product should be
items.explanation: Testing against the requirements checks whether the design actually does what was asked, which is how you judge whether it is finished. It says nothing about who worked hardest, the sale price, or color choices; those are unrelated to whether requirements are met.

items.prompt: When a test against a requirement fails, what should you do next?
choices: Ship the product anyway, since testing is only a formality
Iterate: learn from the failure and refine the design, then test again
Delete the requirement so the test passes
Assume late changes are just as cheap, so wait until the very end
items.explanation: A failed test is feedback that sends you around the loop again to refine and retest. Shipping anyway ignores the problem, deleting the requirement hides it rather than meeting the real need, and assuming late changes are cheap is false because cost of change rises over time.

items.prompt: In the V-model, what happens on the way DOWN the left side versus UP the right side?
choices: Down is testing; up is planning
Both sides are only about choosing a color scheme
There is no order; the sides mean nothing
Down is planning and design; up is building and then verifying with tests
items.explanation: The V-model goes down the left through planning and design, reaches build at the bottom, then comes up the right verifying with tests. Testing is not on the way down, the model is about design and verification rather than color, and the two sides do carry a clear meaning and order.

items.prompt: The V-model captures one simple rule about requirements and tests. What is it?
choices: Tests are optional and only run if there is spare time
Every requirement should have a matching test that checks it
One test at the end can cover every requirement at once
Requirements and tests have nothing to do with each other
items.explanation: The core idea is traceability: each requirement should have a matching test that verifies it does what was asked. Tests are not optional extras, a single end-of-project test rarely proves every requirement, and requirements and tests are tightly linked, not unrelated.

items.prompt: Editing an essay through several drafts, each better because the last revealed what to fix, is a good picture of what design idea?
choices: A one-shot straight line with no revisions
Iteration through build-test-learn feedback loops
Proof that you should never get feedback on your work
A reason to finish everything before checking it once
items.explanation: Each draft improving on the last is iteration powered by feedback, the same build-test-learn loop used in design. It is not a single straight pass, it relies on feedback rather than avoiding it, and it shows checking work along the way instead of only at the end.

items.prompt: What is a 'design review'?
choices: A pause to let others check your work before you commit to it
A test that runs automatically after shipping
A rule that forbids changing the design ever again
A guess about how much customers will like the product
items.explanation: A design review is when you pause and have others check your work before committing, catching problems while they are still cheap to fix. It is not a post-ship automatic test, not a ban on future changes, and not a guess about customer reactions.
